question: Why are users in Texas underspending compared to California?
sql: SELECT u.state, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT u.id), 2) AS spend_per_user, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) / COUNT(DISTINCT u.id), 2) AS orders_per_user, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id), 2) AS avg_order_value FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u LEFT JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi ON oi.user_id = u.id AND oi.status IN ('Complete', 'Shipped') WHERE u.state IN ('Texas', 'California') GROUP BY u.state
analyst_notes: 'Why' questions are never one query. Decompose the gap: spend per user = order frequency x average order value, so first find WHICH factor drives the difference, then drill into that factor (category mix, age demographics, traffic_source cohorts). Compare per-user rates, never raw state totals - state populations differ. Conclude with the dominant factor and note what was ruled out.
author: artem
